title: The day Steve Jobs dissed me in a keynote | Derek Sivers
excerpt: Derek Sivers official site. Thoughts on philosophy, culture, self-improvement. Author of Useful Not True, How to Live, Hell Yeah or No, Anything You Want.
content_html:
The day Steve Jobs dissed me in a keynote

2010-11-11

In May 2003, Apple invited me to their headquarters to discuss getting CD Baby’s catalog into the iTunes Music Store.

iTunes had just launched two weeks before, with only some music from the major labels. Many of us in the music biz were not sure this idea was going to work. Especially those who had seen companies like eMusic do this exact same model for years without big success.

I flew to Cupertino thinking I’d be meeting with one of their marketing or tech people. When I arrived, I found out that about a hundred people from small record labels and distributors had also been invited.

We all went into a little presentation room, not knowing what to expect.

Then out comes Steve Jobs. Whoa! Rock star.

He was in full persuasive presentation mode. Trying to convince all of us to give Apple our entire catalog of music. Talking about iTunes success so far, and all the reasons we should work with Apple.

He made a point of saying, “We want the iTunes Music Store to have every piece of music ever recorded. Even if it’s discontinued or not selling much, we want it all.”

This was huge, because until 2003, independent musicians were always denied access to the big outlets. For Apple to sell all music, not just music from artists who had signed their rights away to a corporation, this was amazing!

Then they showed us the software we’d all have to use to send them each album. The software required us to put the audio CD into a Mac CD-Rom drive, type in all of the album info, song titles and bio, then click [encode] for it to rip, and [upload] when done.

I raised my hand and asked if it was required that we use their software. They said yes.

I asked again, saying we had over 100,000 albums, already ripped as lossless WAV files, with all of the info carefully entered by the artist themselves, ready to send to their servers with their exact specifications.

The Apple guys said, “Sorry, you need to use this software; there is no other way.”

Ugh. That means we have to pull each one of those CDs off of the shelf again, stick it in a Mac, then cut-and-paste every song title into that Mac software. But so be it. If that’s what Apple needs, OK. They said they’d be ready for us to start uploading in the next couple weeks.

I flew home that night, posted my meeting notes on my website, emailed all of my clients to announce the news, and went to sleep.

When I woke, I had furious emails and voicemails from my contact at Apple.

“What the hell are you doing? That meeting was confidential! Take those notes off your site immediately! Our legal department is furious!”

There was no mention of confidentiality at the meeting and no agreement to sign. But I removed my notes from my site immediately, to be nice. All was well, or so I thought.

Apple emailed us the iTunes Music Store contract. We immediately signed it and returned it the same day. I started building the system to deliver everyone’s music to iTunes.

I decided we’d have to charge $40 for this service, to cover our bandwidth and payroll costs of pulling each CD out of the warehouse, entering all the info, digitizing, uploading, and putting it back in the warehouse.

5000 musicians signed up in advance, each paying $40. That $200,000 helped pay for the extra equipment and people needed to make this happen.

Within two weeks, we got contacted by Rhapsody, Yahoo Music, Napster, eMusic, and more — each saying they wanted our entire catalog. Yes! Awesome!

Maybe you can’t appreciate this now, but the summer of 2003 was the biggest turning point that independent music has ever had. Until that point, almost no big business would sell independent music.

By iTunes saying they wanted everything, then their competitors needing to keep up, we were in! Since the summer of 2003, all musicians everywhere can sell all their music in almost every outlet online. Do you realize how amazing that is?

But there was one problem. iTunes wasn’t getting back to us. Yahoo, Rhapsody, Napster and the rest were all up and running. But iTunes wasn’t returning our signed contract. Was it because I posted my meeting notes? Had I pissed-off Steve Jobs?

Nobody at Apple would say anything. It had been months. My musicians were getting impatient and angry. I gave optimistic apologies, but I was starting to get worried, too.

A month later, Steve Jobs did a special worldwide simulcast keynote speech about iTunes. People had been criticizing iTunes for having less music than the competition. They had 400,000 songs while Rhapsody and Napster had over 2 million songs. (Over 500,000 of those were from CD Baby.)

Four minutes in, he said something that made my pounding heart sink to my burning stomach:

“This number could have easily been much higher, if we wanted to let in every song. But we realize that record companies do a great service. They edit! Did you know that if you and I record a song, for $40 we can pay a few of the services to get it on their site, through some intermediaries? We can be on Rhapsody and all these other guys for $40? Well we don’t want to let that stuff on our site! So we’ve had to edit it. And these are 400,000 quality songs.”

Whoa! Wow. Steve Jobs just dissed me hard! I’m the only one charging $40. That was me he’s referring to!

Shit. OK. That’s that. Steve changed his mind. No independents on iTunes. You heard the man.

I hated the position this put me in. Ever since I started my company in 1998, I had been offering an excellent service. I could make promises and keep them, because I was in full control. Now, for the first time, I had promised something that was out of my control.

So it was time to do the right thing, no matter how much it hurt. I decided to refund everybody’s $40, with my deepest apologies. With 5000 musicians signed up, that meant I was refunding $200,000. Ouch. Since we couldn’t promise anything, I couldn’t charge money in good conscience.

I removed all mention of iTunes from my site. I removed the $40 cost. I changed the language to say we can’t promise anything. I emailed everyone to let them know what had happened. I decided to make it a free service from that point on.

The very next day, we got our signed contract back from Apple, along with upload instructions. Unbelievable.

We asked, “Why now?”, but got no answer.

Whatever. Fucking Apple.

We started encoding and uploading immediately. I quietly added iTunes back to the list of companies on our site. But I never again promised a customer that I could do something beyond my full control.

blocks.html_en: The day Steve Jobs dissed me in a keynote

sentences.en: The day Steve Jobs dissed me in a keynote
sentences.zh: 史蒂夫·乔布斯在主题演讲中嘲笑我的那天

blocks.html_en: In May 2003, Apple invited me to their headquarters to discuss getting CD Baby’s catalog into the iTunes Music Store.

sentences.en: In May 2003, Apple invited me to their headquarters to discuss getting CD Baby’s catalog into the iTunes Music Store.
sentences.zh: 2003 年 5 月，Apple 邀请我到他们的总部讨论将 CD Baby 的目录纳入 iTunes 音乐商店。

blocks.html_en: iTunes had just launched two weeks before, with only some music from the major labels. Many of us in the music biz were not sure this idea was going to work. Especially those who had seen companies like eMusic do this exact same model for years without big success.

sentences.en: iTunes had just launched two weeks before, with only some music from the major labels.
sentences.zh: iTunes 两周前刚刚推出，只有一些来自主要唱片公司的音乐。

sentences.en: Many of us in the music biz were not sure this idea was going to work.
sentences.zh: 我们音乐界的许多人都不确定这个想法是否可行。

sentences.en: Especially those who had seen companies like eMusic do this exact same model for years without big success.
sentences.zh: 尤其是那些见过 eMusic 这样的公司多年来一直采用完全相同的模式但没有取得巨大成功的人。

blocks.html_en: I flew to Cupertino thinking I’d be meeting with one of their marketing or tech people. When I arrived, I found out that about a hundred people from small record labels and distributors had also been invited.

sentences.en: I flew to Cupertino thinking I’d be meeting with one of their marketing or tech people.
sentences.zh: 我飞往库比蒂诺，以为我会见他们的一位营销或技术人员。

sentences.en: When I arrived, I found out that about a hundred people from small record labels and distributors had also been invited.
sentences.zh: 当我到达时，我发现大约一百位来自小唱片公司和发行商的人也被邀请了。

blocks.html_en: We all went into a little presentation room, not knowing what to expect.

sentences.en: We all went into a little presentation room, not knowing what to expect.
sentences.zh: 我们都走进了一个小展示室，不知道会发生什么。

blocks.html_en: Then out comes Steve Jobs. Whoa! Rock star.

sentences.en: Then out comes Steve Jobs.
sentences.zh: 然后史蒂夫·乔布斯出来了。

sentences.en: Whoa!
sentences.zh: 哇！

sentences.en: Rock star.
sentences.zh: 摇滚明星。

blocks.html_en: He was in full persuasive presentation mode. Trying to convince all of us to give Apple our entire catalog of music. Talking about iTunes success so far, and all the reasons we should work with Apple.

sentences.en: He was in full persuasive presentation mode.
sentences.zh: 他处于完全有说服力的演讲模式。

sentences.en: Trying to convince all of us to give Apple our entire catalog of music.
sentences.zh: 试图说服我们所有人向苹果提供我们的整个音乐目录。

sentences.en: Talking about iTunes success so far, and all the reasons we should work with Apple.
sentences.zh: 谈论 iTunes 迄今为止的成功，以及我们应该与 Apple 合作的所有原因。

blocks.html_en: He made a point of saying, “We want the iTunes Music Store to have every piece of music ever recorded. Even if it’s discontinued or not selling much, we want it all.”

sentences.en: He made a point of saying, “We want the iTunes Music Store to have every piece of music ever recorded.
sentences.zh: 他特意说道：“我们希望 iTunes 音乐商店能够收录有史以来录制的所有音乐作品。

sentences.en: Even if it’s discontinued or not selling much, we want it all.”
sentences.zh: 即使它停产了或者卖得不多，我们也想要这一切。”

blocks.html_en: This was huge, because until 2003, independent musicians were always denied access to the big outlets. For Apple to sell all music, not just music from artists who had signed their rights away to a corporation, this was amazing!

sentences.en: This was huge, because until 2003, independent musicians were always denied access to the big outlets.
sentences.zh: 这是一个巨大的数字，因为直到 2003 年，独立音乐家总是被拒绝进入大型媒体。

sentences.en: For Apple to sell all music, not just music from artists who had signed their rights away to a corporation, this was amazing!
sentences.zh: 对于苹果来说，出售所有音乐，而不仅仅是将权利转让给公司的艺术家的音乐，这真是太棒了！

blocks.html_en: Then they showed us the software we’d all have to use to send them each album. The software required us to put the audio CD into a Mac CD-Rom drive, type in all of the album info, song titles and bio, then click [encode] for it to rip, and [upload] when done.

sentences.en: Then they showed us the software we’d all have to use to send them each album.
sentences.zh: 然后他们向我们展示了我们都必须使用的软件来向他们发送每张专辑。

sentences.en: The software required us to put the audio CD into a Mac CD-Rom drive, type in all of the album info, song titles and bio, then click [encode] for it to rip, and [upload] when done.
sentences.zh: 该软件要求我们将音频 CD 放入 Mac CD-ROM 驱动器中，输入所有专辑信息、歌曲标题和简介，然后单击[编码]进行翻录，完成后单击[上传]。

blocks.html_en: I raised my hand and asked if it was required that we use their software. They said yes.

sentences.en: I raised my hand and asked if it was required that we use their software.
sentences.zh: 我举手问我们是否需要使用他们的软件。

sentences.en: They said yes.
sentences.zh: 他们说是的。

sentences.en: I asked again, saying we had over 100,000 albums, already ripped as lossless WAV files, with all of the info carefully entered by the artist themselves, ready to send to their servers with their exact specifications.
sentences.zh: 我再次询问，说我们有超过 100,000 张专辑，已经被撕成无损 WAV 文件，所有信息都由艺术家自己仔细输入，准备发送到他们的服务器并附有确切的规格。

blocks.html_en: The Apple guys said, “Sorry, you need to use this software; there is no other way.”

sentences.en: The Apple guys said, “Sorry, you need to use this software; there is no other way.”
sentences.zh: 苹果公司的人说：“抱歉，你需要使用这个软件，没有其他办法。”

blocks.html_en: Ugh. That means we have to pull each one of those CDs off of the shelf again, stick it in a Mac, then cut-and-paste every song title into that Mac software. But so be it. If that’s what Apple needs, OK. They said they’d be ready for us to start uploading in the next couple weeks.

sentences.en: Ugh.
sentences.zh: 啊。

sentences.en: That means we have to pull each one of those CDs off of the shelf again, stick it in a Mac, then cut-and-paste every song title into that Mac software.
sentences.zh: 这意味着我们必须再次从架子上取出每张 CD，将其插入 Mac，然后将每首歌曲标题剪切并粘贴到该 Mac 软件中。

sentences.en: But so be it.
sentences.zh: 但就这样吧。

sentences.en: If that’s what Apple needs, OK.
sentences.zh: 如果这就是苹果所需要的，那么好吧。

sentences.en: They said they’d be ready for us to start uploading in the next couple weeks.
sentences.zh: 他们说他们将准备好让我们在接下来的几周内开始上传。

blocks.html_en: I flew home that night, posted my meeting notes on my website, emailed all of my clients to announce the news, and went to sleep.

sentences.en: I flew home that night, posted my meeting notes on my website, emailed all of my clients to announce the news, and went to sleep.
sentences.zh: 那天晚上我飞回家，在我的网站上发布了我的会议记录，通过电子邮件向我所有的客户宣布了这个消息，然后就睡觉了。

blocks.html_en: When I woke, I had furious emails and voicemails from my contact at Apple.

sentences.en: When I woke, I had furious emails and voicemails from my contact at Apple.
sentences.zh: 当我醒来时，我收到了苹果联系人发来的愤怒的电子邮件和语音邮件。

blocks.html_en: “What the hell are you doing? That meeting was confidential! Take those notes off your site immediately! Our legal department is furious!”

sentences.en: “What the hell are you doing?
sentences.zh: “你到底在做什么？

sentences.en: That meeting was confidential!
sentences.zh: 那次会议是保密的！

sentences.en: Take those notes off your site immediately!
sentences.zh: 立即将这些笔记从您的网站上删除！

sentences.en: Our legal department is furious!”
sentences.zh: 我们的法律部门非常愤怒！”

sentences.en: There was no mention of confidentiality at the meeting and no agreement to sign.
sentences.zh: 会议上没有提及保密问题，也没有签署协议。

sentences.en: But I removed my notes from my site immediately, to be nice.
sentences.zh: 但为了表示友善，我立即从网站上删除了我的笔记。

sentences.en: All was well, or so I thought.
sentences.zh: 一切都很好，至少我是这么认为的。

blocks.html_en: Apple emailed us the iTunes Music Store contract. We immediately signed it and returned it the same day. I started building the system to deliver everyone’s music to iTunes.

sentences.en: We immediately signed it and returned it the same day.
sentences.zh: 我们立即签字并于当天退回。

sentences.en: I started building the system to deliver everyone’s music to iTunes.

sentences.en: I decided we’d have to charge $40 for this service, to cover our bandwidth and payroll costs of pulling each CD out of the warehouse, entering all the info, digitizing, uploading, and putting it back in the warehouse.
sentences.zh: 我决定我们必须为这项服务收取 40 美元，以支付我们的带宽和工资成本，包括将每张 CD 从仓库中取出、输入所有信息、数字化、上传并将其放回仓库。

blocks.html_en: 5000 musicians signed up in advance, each paying $40. That $200,000 helped pay for the extra equipment and people needed to make this happen.

sentences.en: 5000 musicians signed up in advance, each paying $40.

sentences.en: That $200,000 helped pay for the extra equipment and people needed to make this happen.
sentences.zh: 这 20 万美元用于支付实现这一目标所需的额外设备和人员。

blocks.html_en: Within two weeks, we got contacted by Rhapsody, Yahoo Music, Napster, eMusic, and more — each saying they wanted our entire catalog. Yes! Awesome!

sentences.en: Within two weeks, we got contacted by Rhapsody, Yahoo Music, Napster, eMusic, and more — each saying they wanted our entire catalog.

sentences.en: Yes!
sentences.zh: 是的！

sentences.en: Awesome!
sentences.zh: 惊人的！

blocks.html_en: Maybe you can’t appreciate this now, but the summer of 2003 was the biggest turning point that independent music has ever had. Until that point, almost no big business would sell independent music.

sentences.zh: 自 2003 年夏天以来，世界各地的所有音乐家都可以在几乎每个在线商店出售他们的所有音乐。

sentences.en: Do you realize how amazing that is?
sentences.zh: 你知道那有多神奇吗？

sentences.zh: 但有一个问题。

sentences.en: iTunes wasn’t getting back to us.
sentences.zh: iTunes 没有回复我们。

sentences.en: Yahoo, Rhapsody, Napster and the rest were all up and running.
sentences.zh: 雅虎、Rhapsody、Napster 和其他公司都已启动并运行。

sentences.en: But iTunes wasn’t returning our signed contract.
sentences.zh: 但 iTunes 没有退还我们签署的合同。

sentences.en: Was it because I posted my meeting notes?
sentences.zh: 是因为我发布了会议记录吗？

sentences.en: Had I pissed-off Steve Jobs?
sentences.zh: 我惹恼了史蒂夫·乔布斯吗？

sentences.zh: 已经好几个月了。

sentences.en: My musicians were getting impatient and angry.
sentences.zh: 我的音乐家们变得不耐烦和愤怒。

sentences.en: I gave optimistic apologies, but I was starting to get worried, too.
sentences.zh: 我乐观地道歉，但我也开始担心。

They had 400,000 songs while Rhapsody and Napster had over 2 million songs. (Over 500,000 of those were from CD Baby.)

sentences.en: A month later, Steve Jobs did a special worldwide simulcast keynote speech about iTunes.
sentences.zh: 一个月后，史蒂夫·乔布斯做了一场关于 iTunes 的特别全球联播主题演讲。

sentences.en: People had been criticizing iTunes for having less music than the competition.
sentences.zh: 人们一直批评 iTunes 的音乐少于竞争对手。

sentences.en: They had 400,000 songs while Rhapsody and Napster had over 2 million songs.
sentences.zh: 他们拥有 40 万首歌曲，而 Rhapsody 和 Napster 则拥有超过 200 万首歌曲。

sentences.en: (Over 500,000 of those were from CD Baby.)
sentences.zh: （其中超过 500,000 个来自 CD Baby。）

blocks.html_en: Four minutes in, he said something that made my pounding heart sink to my burning stomach:

sentences.en: Four minutes in, he said something that made my pounding heart sink to my burning stomach:
sentences.zh: 四分钟后，他说了一些让我狂跳的心沉入灼烧的胃中的话：

blocks.html_en: “This number could have easily been much higher, if we wanted to let in every song. But we realize that record companies do a great service. They edit! Did you know that if you and I record a song, for $40 we can pay a few of the services to get it on their site, through some intermediaries? We can be on Rhapsody and all these other guys for $40? Well we don’t want to let that stuff on our site! So we’ve had to edit it. And these are 400,000 quality songs.”

sentences.en: “This number could have easily been much higher, if we wanted to let in every song.
sentences.zh: “如果我们想让每首歌曲都加入进来，这个数字很可能会更高。

sentences.en: But we realize that record companies do a great service.
sentences.zh: 但我们意识到唱片公司提供了很好的服务。

sentences.en: They edit!
sentences.zh: 他们编辑！

sentences.en: Did you know that if you and I record a song, for $40 we can pay a few of the services to get it on their site, through some intermediaries?
sentences.zh: 您是否知道，如果您和我录制一首歌，我们可以花 40 美元支付一些服务费用，通过一些中介将其发布到他们的网站上？

sentences.en: We can be on Rhapsody and all these other guys for $40?
sentences.zh: 我们可以花 40 美元参加 Rhapsody 和其他所有节目吗？

sentences.en: Well we don’t want to let that stuff on our site!
sentences.zh: 好吧，我们不想让这些东西出现在我们的网站上！

sentences.en: So we’ve had to edit it.
sentences.zh: 所以我们必须对其进行编辑。

sentences.en: And these are 400,000 quality songs.”
sentences.zh: 这些都是 40 万首优质歌曲。”

blocks.html_en: Whoa! Wow. Steve Jobs just dissed me hard! I’m the only one charging $40. That was me he’s referring to!

sentences.en: Whoa!
sentences.zh: 哇！

sentences.en: Wow.
sentences.zh: 哇。

sentences.en: Steve Jobs just dissed me hard!
sentences.zh: 史蒂夫·乔布斯刚刚狠狠地鄙视了我！

sentences.en: I’m the only one charging $40.
sentences.zh: 我是唯一一个收费 40 美元的人。

sentences.en: That was me he’s referring to!
sentences.zh: 他指的就是我！

blocks.html_en: Shit. OK. That’s that. Steve changed his mind. No independents on iTunes. You heard the man.

sentences.en: Shit.
sentences.zh: 拉屎。

sentences.en: OK.
sentences.zh: 好的。

sentences.en: That’s that.
sentences.zh: 就是这样。

sentences.en: Steve changed his mind.
sentences.zh: 史蒂夫改变了主意。

sentences.en: No independents on iTunes.
sentences.zh: iTunes 上没有独立音乐。

sentences.en: You heard the man.
sentences.zh: 你听到了那个男人的话。

blocks.html_en: I hated the position this put me in. Ever since I started my company in 1998, I had been offering an excellent service. I could make promises and keep them, because I was in full control. Now, for the first time, I had promised something that was out of my control.

sentences.en: I hated the position this put me in.
sentences.zh: 我讨厌这让我陷入的境地。

sentences.en: Ever since I started my company in 1998, I had been offering an excellent service.
sentences.zh: 自从我 1998 年创办公司以来，我一直在提供优质的服务。

sentences.en: I could make promises and keep them, because I was in full control.
sentences.zh: 我可以做出承诺并遵守它们，因为我完全掌控一切。

sentences.en: Now, for the first time, I had promised something that was out of my control.
sentences.zh: 现在，我第一次承诺了一些我无法控制的事情。

blocks.html_en: So it was time to do the right thing, no matter how much it hurt. I decided to refund everybody’s $40, with my deepest apologies. With 5000 musicians signed up, that meant I was refunding $200,000. Ouch. Since we couldn’t promise anything, I couldn’t charge money in good conscience.

sentences.en: So it was time to do the right thing, no matter how much it hurt.
sentences.zh: 因此，是时候做正确的事情了，无论这会带来多大的伤害。

sentences.en: I decided to refund everybody’s $40, with my deepest apologies.
sentences.zh: 我决定退还每个人 40 美元，并致以最深切的歉意。

sentences.en: With 5000 musicians signed up, that meant I was refunding $200,000.
sentences.zh: 有 5000 名音乐家注册，这意味着我要退还 20 万美元。

sentences.en: Ouch.
sentences.zh: 哎哟。

sentences.en: Since we couldn’t promise anything, I couldn’t charge money in good conscience.
sentences.zh: 由于我们无法做出任何承诺，因此我无法凭良心收费。

blocks.html_en: I removed all mention of iTunes from my site. I removed the $40 cost. I changed the language to say we can’t promise anything. I emailed everyone to let them know what had happened. I decided to make it a free service from that point on.

sentences.en: I removed all mention of iTunes from my site.
sentences.zh: 我从我的网站上删除了所有提及 iTunes 的内容。

sentences.en: I removed the $40 cost.
sentences.zh: 我去掉了 40 美元的费用。

sentences.en: I changed the language to say we can’t promise anything.
sentences.zh: 我改变了措辞，说我们不能承诺任何事情。

sentences.en: I emailed everyone to let them know what had happened.
sentences.zh: 我给每个人发了电子邮件，让他们知道发生了什么事。

sentences.en: I decided to make it a free service from that point on.
sentences.zh: 我决定从那时起将其设为免费服务。

blocks.html_en: The very next day, we got our signed contract back from Apple, along with upload instructions. Unbelievable.

sentences.en: The very next day, we got our signed contract back from Apple, along with upload instructions.
sentences.zh: 第二天，我们就收到了苹果公司发来的已签署合同以及上传说明。

sentences.en: Unbelievable.
sentences.zh: 难以置信。

blocks.html_en: We asked, “Why now?”, but got no answer.

sentences.en: We asked, “Why now?”, but got no answer.
sentences.zh: 我们问：“为什么现在？”，但没有得到答案。

blocks.html_en: Whatever. Fucking Apple.

sentences.en: Whatever.
sentences.zh: 任何。

sentences.en: Fucking Apple.
sentences.zh: 该死的苹果。

blocks.html_en: We started encoding and uploading immediately. I quietly added iTunes back to the list of companies on our site. But I never again promised a customer that I could do something beyond my full control.

sentences.en: We started encoding and uploading immediately.
sentences.zh: 我们立即开始编码和上传。

sentences.en: I quietly added iTunes back to the list of companies on our site.
sentences.zh: 我悄悄地将 iTunes 添加回我们网站上的公司列表中。

sentences.en: But I never again promised a customer that I could do something beyond my full control.
sentences.zh: 但我再也没有向客户承诺我可以做一些超出我完全控制范围的事情。

content_html_zh:
史蒂夫·乔布斯在主题演讲中嘲笑我的那天

2010-11-11

2003 年 5 月，Apple 邀请我到他们的总部讨论将 CD Baby 的目录纳入 iTunes 音乐商店。

iTunes 两周前刚刚推出，只有一些来自主要唱片公司的音乐。 我们音乐界的许多人都不确定这个想法是否可行。 尤其是那些见过 eMusic 这样的公司多年来一直采用完全相同的模式但没有取得巨大成功的人。

我飞往库比蒂诺，以为我会见他们的一位营销或技术人员。 当我到达时，我发现大约一百位来自小唱片公司和发行商的人也被邀请了。

我们都走进了一个小展示室，不知道会发生什么。

然后史蒂夫·乔布斯出来了。 哇！ 摇滚明星。

他处于完全有说服力的演讲模式。 试图说服我们所有人向苹果提供我们的整个音乐目录。 谈论 iTunes 迄今为止的成功，以及我们应该与 Apple 合作的所有原因。

他特意说道：“我们希望 iTunes 音乐商店能够收录有史以来录制的所有音乐作品。 即使它停产了或者卖得不多，我们也想要这一切。”

这是一个巨大的数字，因为直到 2003 年，独立音乐家总是被拒绝进入大型媒体。 对于苹果来说，出售所有音乐，而不仅仅是将权利转让给公司的艺术家的音乐，这真是太棒了！

然后他们向我们展示了我们都必须使用的软件来向他们发送每张专辑。 该软件要求我们将音频 CD 放入 Mac CD-ROM 驱动器中，输入所有专辑信息、歌曲标题和简介，然后单击[编码]进行翻录，完成后单击[上传]。

我举手问我们是否需要使用他们的软件。 他们说是的。

我再次询问，说我们有超过 100,000 张专辑，已经被撕成无损 WAV 文件，所有信息都由艺术家自己仔细输入，准备发送到他们的服务器并附有确切的规格。

苹果公司的人说：“抱歉，你需要使用这个软件，没有其他办法。”

啊。 这意味着我们必须再次从架子上取出每张 CD，将其插入 Mac，然后将每首歌曲标题剪切并粘贴到该 Mac 软件中。 但就这样吧。 如果这就是苹果所需要的，那么好吧。 他们说他们将准备好让我们在接下来的几周内开始上传。

那天晚上我飞回家，在我的网站上发布了我的会议记录，通过电子邮件向我所有的客户宣布了这个消息，然后就睡觉了。

当我醒来时，我收到了苹果联系人发来的愤怒的电子邮件和语音邮件。

“你到底在做什么？ 那次会议是保密的！ 立即将这些笔记从您的网站上删除！ 我们的法律部门非常愤怒！”

会议上没有提及保密问题，也没有签署协议。 但为了表示友善，我立即从网站上删除了我的笔记。 一切都很好，至少我是这么认为的。

Apple 通过电子邮件向我们发送了 iTunes 音乐商店合同。 我们立即签字并于当天退回。 我开始构建一个系统，将每个人的音乐传送到 iTunes。

我决定我们必须为这项服务收取 40 美元，以支付我们的带宽和工资成本，包括将每张 CD 从仓库中取出、输入所有信息、数字化、上传并将其放回仓库。

5000 名音乐家提前报名，每人支付 40 美元。 这 20 万美元用于支付实现这一目标所需的额外设备和人员。

两周之内，Rhapsody、雅虎音乐、Napster、eMusic 等公司联系了我们，他们都表示想要我们的整个目录。 是的！ 惊人的！

也许你现在还无法体会到这一点，但2003年夏天是独立音乐有史以来最大的转折点。 在那之前，几乎没有大企业会销售独立音乐。

iTunes 说他们想要一切，然后他们的竞争对手需要跟上，我们就加入了！ 自 2003 年夏天以来，世界各地的所有音乐家都可以在几乎每个在线商店出售他们的所有音乐。 你知道那有多神奇吗？

但有一个问题。 iTunes 没有回复我们。 雅虎、Rhapsody、Napster 和其他公司都已启动并运行。 但 iTunes 没有退还我们签署的合同。 是因为我发布了会议记录吗？ 我惹恼了史蒂夫·乔布斯吗？

苹果公司没有人会说什么。 已经好几个月了。 我的音乐家们变得不耐烦和愤怒。 我乐观地道歉，但我也开始担心。

一个月后，史蒂夫·乔布斯做了一场关于 iTunes 的特别全球联播主题演讲。 人们一直批评 iTunes 的音乐少于竞争对手。 他们拥有 40 万首歌曲，而 Rhapsody 和 Napster 则拥有超过 200 万首歌曲。 （其中超过 500,000 个来自 CD Baby。）

四分钟后，他说了一些让我狂跳的心沉入灼烧的胃中的话：

“如果我们想让每首歌曲都加入进来，这个数字很可能会更高。 但我们意识到唱片公司提供了很好的服务。 他们编辑！ 您是否知道，如果您和我录制一首歌，我们可以花 40 美元支付一些服务费用，通过一些中介将其发布到他们的网站上？ 我们可以花 40 美元参加 Rhapsody 和其他所有节目吗？ 好吧，我们不想让这些东西出现在我们的网站上！ 所以我们必须对其进行编辑。 这些都是 40 万首优质歌曲。”

哇！ 哇。 史蒂夫·乔布斯刚刚狠狠地鄙视了我！ 我是唯一一个收费 40 美元的人。 他指的就是我！

拉屎。 好的。 就是这样。 史蒂夫改变了主意。 iTunes 上没有独立音乐。 你听到了那个男人的话。

我讨厌这让我陷入的境地。 自从我 1998 年创办公司以来，我一直在提供优质的服务。 我可以做出承诺并遵守它们，因为我完全掌控一切。 现在，我第一次承诺了一些我无法控制的事情。

因此，是时候做正确的事情了，无论这会带来多大的伤害。 我决定退还每个人 40 美元，并致以最深切的歉意。 有 5000 名音乐家注册，这意味着我要退还 20 万美元。 哎哟。 由于我们无法做出任何承诺，因此我无法凭良心收费。

我从我的网站上删除了所有提及 iTunes 的内容。 我去掉了 40 美元的费用。 我改变了措辞，说我们不能承诺任何事情。 我给每个人发了电子邮件，让他们知道发生了什么事。 我决定从那时起将其设为免费服务。

第二天，我们就收到了苹果公司发来的已签署合同以及上传说明。 难以置信。

我们问：“为什么现在？”，但没有得到答案。

任何。 该死的苹果。

我们立即开始编码和上传。 我悄悄地将 iTunes 添加回我们网站上的公司列表中。 但我再也没有向客户承诺我可以做一些超出我完全控制范围的事情。
